title: Convolution theorem
content:
In mathematics, the convolution theorem states that under suitable conditions the Fourier transform of a convolution of two functions (or signals) is the pointwise product of their Fourier transforms. More generally, convolution in one domain (e.g., time domain) equals point-wise multiplication in the other domain (e.g., frequency domain). Other versions of the convolution theorem are applicable to various Fourier-related transforms. Functions of a continuous-variable. Consider two functions formula_1 and formula_2 with Fourier transform s formula_3 and formula_4: where formula_6 denotes the Fourier transform operator.

The transform may be normalized in other ways, in which case constant scaling factors (typically formula_7 or formula_8) will appear in the convolution theorem below. The convolution of formula_9 and formula_10 is defined by: In this context the asterisk denotes convolution, instead of standard multiplication. The tensor product symbol formula_12 is sometimes used instead. It can be extended to the Fourier transform of abstract harmonic analysis defined over locally compact abelian groups. Periodic convolution (Fourier series coefficients). By a derivation similar to Eq.1, there is an analogous theorem for sequences, such as samples of two continuous functions, where now formula_6 denotes the <a href="discrete-time%20Fourier%20transform" discrete-time Fourier transform</a (DTFT) operator. The discrete convolution: is also formula_60-periodic, and is called a <a href="periodic%20convolution" periodic convolution</a. But when the non-zero portion of the formula_74 or formula_75 sequence is equal or longer than formula_76 some distortion is inevitable. Such is the case when the formula_77 sequence is obtained by directly sampling the DTFT of the infinitely long impulse response. Therefore, the equivalence of (5a) and (5b) requires: <br We can also verify the inverse DTFT of (5b): Convolution theorem for inverse Fourier transform. There is also a convolution theorem for the inverse Fourier transform: so that Convolution theorem for tempered distributions. The convolution theorem extends to tempered distributions. Here, formula_9 is an arbitrary tempered distribution (e.g.

the Dirac comb) but formula_97 must be "rapidly decreasing" towards formula_98 and formula_99 in order to guarantee the existence of both, convolution and multiplication product. Equivalently, if formula_100 is a smooth "slowly growing" ordinary function, it guarantees the existence of both, multiplication and convolution product. In particular, every compactly supported tempered distribution, such as the Dirac Delta, is "rapidly decreasing". Equivalently, bandlimited functions, such as the function that is constantly formula_101 are smooth "slowly growing" ordinary functions.

If, for example, formula_102 is the Dirac comb both equations yield the Poisson Summation Formula and if, furthermore, formula_103 is the Dirac delta then formula_104 is constantly one and these equations yield the Dirac comb identity.
